objective: Build provider-sponsored, customer-controlled verified API migrations that turn authoritative provider change intent into evidence-backed draft PRs for explicitly authorized customer repositories.
proven_findings: The Go CLI/config/result/exit foundation is implemented.
Workflow, evidence, cassette, trace, proof, redaction, action-boundary, and command-result schemas are executable.
Local OpenAPI/docs intake produces structured references, fingerprints, deprecation findings, and replacement hints.

Provider enrollment and provider-status refresh, explicit authorization issue/revoke, authenticated receipt acknowledgement, host isolation, recorder, replay execution, live verification, reporting, migration analysis, patching, GitHub delivery, and live agent evaluation are not implemented.
Recognized but unimplemented commands currently return a generic pass and must fail closed first.

facts: The API provider can own authoritative change intent and fund a campaign.
The API consumer owns repository access, execution, disclosure, review, and merge.
Public API artifacts can seed benchmarks without establishing provider demand or authorization.
inferences: The strongest first product is provider-sponsored and customer-controlled, not conventional one-principal sell-side automation.
Consumer trust and consent are the principal activation risk; patch generation alone does not establish commercial viability.

resolved.value: Go CLI with parser-backed TypeScript/Node analysis in local or customer CI execution
customer-authorized draft GitHub PR; no default-branch write or auto-merge
no model-provider adapter required for the deterministic MVP

M0-M4 may use pinned license-compatible public API docs, OpenAPI descriptions, SDK releases, migration guides, and synthetic fixtures; these are untrusted engineering inputs, not provider or demand evidence
Consumer authorization: campaign acceptance creates only a no-authority request; explicit consumer-signed issue and revoke actions control every product grant
Trust freshness: every side effect uses a signed in-age offline provider-status snapshot or an exact endpoint read under provider_trust_status_read that sends no repository or consumer data

Sponsored connection meter: only a consumer-signed, invitation-bound minimal receipt whose signer is provider-authenticated and whose one-repository unit has a provider-signed deduplicated acknowledgement counts; richer attestation is separate and optional
Distribution posture: the design-partner package is explicitly licensed and integrity-signed but not called OSS; public OSS/self-serve has a separate release gate

reviewed_surfaces: all authored product and operating docs
historical Factory plan and closure evidence
bounds: No provider demand is inferred from public documentation.
No unimplemented command or planned runtime is described as complete.

failed_approaches: Generic agent-readiness evaluation is superseded as the commercial wedge.
Buy-side monitoring of every vendor is deferred because public documentation lacks authoritative semantic intent and distribution.
Provider-controlled repository automation is rejected because payment does not convey customer authority.
constraints: Fail closed on ambiguous intent, authorization, patch scope, or proof.
Keep provider and consumer data planes separate.
Use deterministic transformations before any model-assisted fallback.
Preserve Factory lifecycle and item-level closure.
unknowns: Which qualified API provider and upcoming migration will close M2.5 and anchor the first campaign.
Which customer maintainers will authorize repositories and sandbox access.
Which of the three supported change classes will dominate the first real campaign.
open_questions: M2.5 must resolve provider identity, packet content, trust enrollment and operational status channel, mock or sandbox endpoint, provider acknowledgement key and receipt exchange, authenticated consumer receipt-signer binding, eligible customer cohort and host-isolation compatibility, one-unit connection-receipt acknowledgement, irreversible-disclosure consent, confidentiality, distribution terms, and paid pilot terms before M5 can begin.
assumptions: The first provider has an official TypeScript SDK, deterministic mock or sandbox, and a reachable managed integration cohort.
The first customer repositories permit local or customer-CI execution and draft GitHub PR creation after explicit approval.
Three deterministic change classes cover enough of the first campaign to test the commercial thesis.
decision_points: Stop before M8 without an exact non-production sandbox grant.
Stop before M9 without exact remote-branch and GitHub draft-PR grants; a minimal campaign receipt and richer provider attestation remain separate grants.
Stop before M5 until M2.5 closes with a qualified provider and frozen eligible cohort.
Reframe if provider recruitment, customer consent, deterministic coverage, or willingness-to-pay gates fail.
risk_flags: host command escape or unsupported platform
unauthenticated receipt signer, unit duplication, replay, or over-disclosure
scope_risks: Expanding beyond TypeScript/Node, direct SDK use, GitHub, and three deterministic mappings before the benchmark is green.
Building a hosted campaign dashboard before a provider commits a real migration and customer cohort.
Treating technical fixture success as commercial validation.
system_model_summary: The provider campaign plane supplies immutable change intent and signed current-status evidence, and receives only a separately authorized minimal connection receipt or richer consented attestation. The consumer execution plane owns source, credentials, host-isolated commands, patches, evidence, disclosure, and merge. Lumyn mediates through explicit consumer-signed issue/revoke actions, revocable capability grants, and evidence-bound draft PR delivery.

runtime_pins.toolchain_version: go1.26.5; exact Node, npm, registry-or-snapshot, package-integrity, and toolchain pins before package-lock mutation
runtime_pins.dependency_policy: standard library first; pinned Go modules or a bounded TypeScript parser runtime only with task evidence and license review
runtime_pins.distribution_target: explicitly licensed and integrity-signed design-partner binary/source package for customer-controlled local or CI execution; public OSS/self-serve and Homebrew only after the separate license, security, contribution, support, vulnerability-response, and release-integrity gate
runtime_pins.provider_policy: API-provider change authority is a signed declarative packet whose current trust state is revalidated before every local, sandbox, or remote side effect against a consumer-pinned provider trust root and verified provider-to-package ownership binding, including issuer key, issue time, audience, expiry, rotation, revocation, withdrawal, and replay checks; freshness comes only from a signed offline provider-status snapshot inside the pinned maximum age or an exact endpoint read under provider_trust_status_read, with no repository or consumer data in the request; no model-provider adapter is required for the MVP
runtime_pins.artifact_namespace: .factory/artifacts contains only Factory evidence and separately consented aggregate/hash-only public pilot evidence; private product runtime artifacts use an explicitly configured consumer-controlled root outside the checkout and public source repository; provider export and public commit are irreversible disclosure boundaries, so revocation blocks future sharing and deletes only Lumyn-controlled private copies
runtime_pins.live_work_policy: offline by default; Factory worker approval, credential, and network grants govern implementation work only and never substitute for separate consumer-signed Lumyn authorization; Lumyn revalidates the private bundle and current provider-status evidence immediately before each repository write, host-isolated command, provider-status read, package-registry read, sandbox payload/network/credential action, minimal campaign receipt, provider attestation, remote branch write, GitHub PR, artifact retention, or artifact deletion side effect

deprecated_worker_aliases.migration_behavior: block active task packets until required_worker_chain is migrated

drift_triggers: provider packet trust or status freshness
